title: `itertools.zip_longest` stores NULL in GC-tracked `ittuple` when iterators exhaust
author: devdanzin

comments.author: serhiy-storchaka
comments.body: As you know, segfault was fixed in #146056. So what is the issue again?

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
I assumed having `NULL` accessible wouldn't be desirable. Seems to be the case:
```python
import itertools
import gc

zl = itertools.zip_longest(iter([1]), iter([1, 2, 3]))
next(zl)  # (1, 1) — both iterators alive
next(zl)  # (None, 2) — first iterator exhausted, ittuple[0] set to NULL

# Expose the internal ittuple via gc.get_referents
refs = gc.get_referents(zl)
for r in refs:
    if isinstance(r, tuple):
        print(repr(r))
        break  
print(r[0])
```

Backtrace:
```
Program received signal SIGSEGV, Segmentation fault.
0x0000555555caa089 in _Py_atomic_load_uint32_relaxed (obj=0xc) at ./Include/cpython/pyatomic_gcc.h:367

    argcount=argcount@entry=0, kwnames=kwnames@entry=0x0) at Python/ceval.c:2179
#9  0x0000555555ea80db in PyEval_EvalCode (co=co@entry=0x7bffb63e7a90, globals=globals@entry=0x7bffb670ca90, locals=locals@entry=0x7bffb670ca90) at Python/ceval.c:686

#22 0x00007ffff7c2a628 in __libc_start_main_impl (main=0x5555558e6c70 <main>, argc=2, argv=0x7fffffffbee8, init=<optimized out>, fini=<optimized out>, rtld_fini=<optimized out>,
    stack_end=0x7fffffffbed8) at ../csu/libc-start.c:360
#23 0x00005555557fd525 in _start ()
```

Edit: accessing `NULL` in the list that contains it also crashes using the reproducer in https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/146056. So it seems any container displaying a `NULL` could be susceptible to this, right?

comments.author: manueljacob
comments.body: The segfault for the code in https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/148181#issue-4213002125 was fixed by commit 0f2246b1553f401da5ade47e0fd1c80ad7a8dfa5, but the code in https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/148181#issuecomment-4194254296 still causes a segfault on current main (commit a0c57a8d17eb0f5c4e620d83a13a47cf4d85e76f).

comments.author: sunmy2019
comments.body: Code in https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/148181#issuecomment-4194254296 also crashes in non-free-threading build.

comments.author: sunmy2019
serhiy-storchaka
comments.body:
I am not sure that we can guarantee that all objects exposed to the garbage collector are fully functional.

Although, using a tuple to store iterators and freeing exhausted iterators is an implementation detail. We have many other options, for example:

* Do not free exhausted iterators.
* Use raw array instead of tuple. It will need explicit loops in tp_traverse, tp_clear and destructor.
* Make the tuple untrackable. It will likely need explicit loops in tp_traverse and tp_clear.

cc @rhettinger

comments.author: sergey-miryanov
comments.body:
> I am not sure that we can guarantee that all objects exposed to the garbage collector are fully functional.

They shouldn't be fully functional, but they should be properly initialized. At least, all fields that used in `tp_traverse` or `tp_clear` shouldn't be NULL or should be checked before visit.

Only exclusion is a PyTuple, which allowed to have NULL elements.

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
I got curious about other ways to trigger the crash, maybe these can be used for tests:
```python
import itertools
import gc

import array
import operator

zl = itertools.zip_longest([1], [1], [1], [1], [1], [1, 2, 3])
next(zl)
next(zl)
refs = gc.get_referents(zl)
for r in refs:
    if isinstance(r, tuple):
        print(repr(r))
        break

operator.itemgetter(0)(r)  # segfault via `itemgetter_call_impl`
r.index(1)  # SystemError: Objects/object.c:1106: bad argument to internal function (also `1 in r`)
array.array('i', *r)  # segfault via `list_extend_fast`
sum(*r)  # segfault via `PyObject_GetIter`
r[0:-1]  # segfault via `PyTuple_FromArray`
r * 2  # segfault via `tuple_repeat`
hash(r)  # segfault via `tuple_hash`
"%s %s" % r  # Fatal Python error: _Py_CheckSlotResult: Slot % of type str failed without setting an exception
r += r  # segfault via `_PyTuple_Concat`
```

comments.author: rhettinger
comments.body:
> I am not sure that we can guarantee that all objects exposed to the garbage collector are fully functional.

I believe that has been established multiple times.

> refs = gc.get_referents(zl)

Historically, we won't deem an issue to be a bug if it depends on GC introspection to break the internal invariants.  This isn't a problem that regular users encounter and it is difficult to prevent in the general case.

See also:  `Lib/test/crashers/gc_inspection.py`

I'm closing this for now, but it can be reopened someone can find a non-gc related exploit through the normal public API for `itertools.zip_longest`.
